subject: physics
question_text: Which one of the following has the dimensions of pressure?
solution_text: Pressure is defined as Force per unit Area. Thus, \([P] = \frac{[F]}{[A]} = \frac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [M L^{-1} T^{-2}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: The dimensions of \(RC\), where \(R\) is resistance and \(C\) is capacitance, are the same as that of:
solution_text: The product of resistance \(R\) and capacitance \(C\) is the time constant \(\tau\) of an RC circuit. Mathematically, \([RC] = \left[\frac{V}{I}\right] \times \left[\frac{Q}{V}\right] = \left[\frac{Q}{I}\right] = [T]\).

subject: physics
question_text: Dimensions of the universal gravitational constant \(G\) are:
solution_text: From Newton's law of gravitation, \(F = \frac{G m_1 m_2}{r^2}\), so \(G = \frac{F r^2}{m_1 m_2}\). Substituting dimensions: \([G] = \frac{[M L T^{-2}][L^2]}{[M^2]} = [M^{-1} L^3 T^{-2}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: The dimensional formula of magnetic flux is:
solution_text: Magnetic flux \(\phi = B \cdot A\). Since the magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor is \(F = I L B\), we have \([B] = \frac{[F]}{[I][L]} = \frac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[A][L]} = [M T^{-2} A^{-1}]\). Therefore, \([\phi] = [M T^{-2} A^{-1}][L^2] = [M L^2 T^{-2} A^{-1}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: The dimensions of Planck's constant and angular momentum are respectively:
solution_text: Planck's constant \(h = \frac{E}{\nu} = \frac{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]} = [M L^2 T^{-1}]\). Angular momentum \(L = m v r = [M][L T^{-1}][L] = [M L^2 T^{-1}]\). Thus, they share identical dimensional formulas.

subject: physics
question_text: A photon is a quantum of radiation with energy \(E = h\nu\), where \(\nu\) is frequency and \(h\) is Planck’s constant. The dimensions of \(h\) are the same as that of:
solution_text: The dimension of Planck's constant is \([M L^2 T^{-1}]\). Angular impulse is equal to the change in angular momentum, which has dimensions \([\tau \cdot dt] = [M L^2 T^{-2}][T] = [M L^2 T^{-1}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: The pair having the same dimensions is:
solution_text: Work has dimensions \([M L^2 T^{-2}]\) and torque (Force \(\times\) perpendicular distance) also has dimensions \([M L T^{-2}][L] = [M L^2 T^{-2}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: The dimensional formula \([M L^3 T^{-3} A^{-2}]\) (or expressed using charge \(Q\) as \([M L^3 T^{-1} Q^{-2}]\)) represents which physical quantity?
solution_text: Resistivity \(\rho = R \frac{A}{L}\). Since Resistance \(R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{W}{Q I} = [M L^2 T^{-3} A^{-2}]\), multiplying by length dimensions \(\left[\frac{L^2}{L}\right] = [L]\) gives \([\rho] = [M L^3 T^{-3} A^{-2}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: \([M L^2 T^{-3}]\) is the dimensional formula of:
solution_text: Power is defined as Work done per unit Time. Therefore, \([\text{Power}] = \frac{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}{[T]} = [M L^2 T^{-3}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: If \(R\) and \(L\) represent resistance and self-inductance respectively, which of the following combinations has the dimensions of frequency?
solution_text: The time constant of an LR circuit is given by \(\tau = \frac{L}{R}\). Frequency is the reciprocal of the time constant, so frequency has dimensions of \(\frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{R}{L}\).

subject: physics
question_text: Which of the following ratios expresses pressure?
solution_text: The dimensions of Energy per unit Volume are \(\frac{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}{[L^3]} = [M L^{-1} T^{-2}]\), which is identical to the dimensional formula of pressure.

subject: physics
question_text: The dimensions of the interatomic force constant are:
solution_text: Interatomic force constant \(K = \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{length}}\). Thus, \([K] = \frac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[L]} = [M T^{-2}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: In a particular system, the units of length, mass and time are chosen to be 10 cm, 10 g and 0.1 sec respectively. The unit of force in this system will be equivalent to:
solution_text: Dimensions of force are \([M^1 L^1 T^{-2}]\). Using \(n_2 = n_1 \left[\frac{M_1}{M_2}\right]^1 \left[\frac{L_1}{L_2}\right]^1 \left[\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right]^{-2}\), to find standard Newtons equivalent for 1 unit of this new system: \(1 \times \left(\frac{10\,\text{g}}{1000\,\text{g}}\right) \times \left(\frac{10\,\text{cm}}{100\,\text{cm}}\right) \times \left(\frac{0.1\,\text{s}}{1\,\text{s}}\right)^{-2} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^2 = 0.1\,\text{N}\).

subject: physics
question_text: If momentum (p), area (A) and time (T) are taken to be fundamental quantities, then energy has the dimensional formula:
solution_text: Let \(E \propto p^a A^b T^c\). Substituting dimensions: \([M L^2 T^{-2}] = [M L T^{-1}]^a [L^2]^b [T]^c = M^a L^{a+2b} T^{-a+c}\). Equating powers: \(a = 1\); \(a + 2b = 2 \implies 1 + 2b = 2 \implies b = 1/2\); \(-a + c = -2 \implies -1 + c = -2 \implies c = -1\). Hence, \([E] = [p A^{1/2} T^{-1}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: If units of length, mass and force are chosen as fundamental units, the dimensions of time would be:
solution_text: From \(F = m a = m \frac{L}{T^2}\), rearranging gives \(T^2 = \frac{M L}{F} = M L F^{-1}\). Taking the square root on both sides provides \([T] = [M^{1/2} L^{1/2} F^{-1/2}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: If speed of light (c), acceleration due to gravity (g) and pressure (P) are taken as fundamental units, the dimensions of gravitational constant (G) are:
solution_text: Let \(G = c^x g^y P^z\). Substituting standard dimensions: \([M^{-1} L^3 T^{-2}] = [L T^{-1}]^x [L T^{-2}]^y [M L^{-1} T^{-2}]^z = M^z L^{x+y-z} T^{-x-2y-2z}\). Equating powers: \(z = -1\); from \(-x-2y-2z = -2\) and \(x+y-z = 3\), solving the system gives \(x = 0\) and \(y = 2\). Thus, \([G] = [c^0 g^2 P^{-1}]\).

subject: physics
question_text: If energy (E), velocity (V) and force (F) be taken as fundamental quantity, then what are the dimensions of mass?
solution_text: Kinetic energy relation shows \(E = \frac{1}{2}m V^2 \implies m \propto \frac{E}{V^2} = [E V^{-2}]\). Alternatively, testing components shows force is redundant since energy and velocity uniquely resolve mass.

subject: physics
question_text: If area (A), velocity (V) and density (ρ) are taken as fundamental quantities, the dimensional formula for force is:
solution_text: Let \(F = A^x V^y \rho^z\). Substituting dimensions: \([M L T^{-2}] = [L^2]^x [L T^{-1}]^y [M L^{-3}]^z = M^z L^{2x+y-3z} T^{-y}\). Comparing powers: \(z = 1\), \(-y = -2 \implies y = 2\), and \(2x + 2 - 3(1) = 1 \implies 2x - 1 = 1 \implies x = 1\). Therefore, \([F] = [A V^2 \rho]\).

subject: physics
question_text: In a vernier calipers, one main scale division is x cm and n divisions of the vernier scale coincide with (n-1) divisions of the main scale. The least count (in cm) of the calipers is:
solution_text: Least count (LC) = 1 MSD - 1 VSD. Given \(n\,\text{VSD} = (n-1)\,\text{MSD} \implies 1\,\text{VSD} = \frac{n-1}{n}\,\text{MSD}\). Thus, \(\text{LC} = 1\,\text{MSD} - \frac{n-1}{n}\,\text{MSD} = \frac{1}{n}\,\text{MSD}\). Since \(1\,\text{MSD} = x\,\text{cm}\), \(\text{LC} = \frac{x}{n}\,\text{cm}\).

subject: physics
question_text: In a vernier caliper, ten smallest divisions of the vernier scale are equal to nine smallest divisions on the main scale. If the smallest division on the main scale is half a millimeter, then the vernier constant is:
solution_text: Vernier constant is another name for Least Count. 1 MSD = 0.5 mm. Given 10 VSD = 9 MSD, Least Count = \(1\,\text{MSD} - 1\,\text{VSD} = 1\,\text{MSD} - \frac{9}{10}\,\text{MSD} = \frac{1}{10}\,\text{MSD} = \frac{0.5\,\text{mm}}{10} = 0.05\,\text{mm}\).
